blocks.text: Chapter 5.
The Psychology of Human Misjudgment I: Incentives to Deprival (Tendencies 1-14)
The ideas in 30 seconds

Incentives are the most underestimated force in human affairs: people unconsciously rationalize whatever their pay structure rewards, so you must fix the incentive system before blaming the people inside it.
The brain rushes to remove doubt and defend its first conclusion, which means early ideas — however arrived at — tend to become permanent unless you deliberately force reconsideration.
Emotions like liking, hating, and envy silently distort perception of facts, and envy in particular drives more misery and bad decisions than greed does.

Reciprocation, fairness instincts, and mere association are hardwired social reflexes that manipulators — from car salesmen to advertisers — exploit systematically; Cialdini's concession experiment tripled compliance using nothing but a retreat from an extreme request.

People chronically overrate themselves (90% of Swedish drivers claim above-average skill), overvalue what they own, believe what they wish were true, and overreact to losses and near-losses far more than to equivalent gains.
Each tendency evolved for good reasons and usually works; misjudgment comes when modern conditions misfire it, so the cure is a checklist of tendencies plus specific antidotes, not willpower.
Never Underestimate Incentives: Reward and Punishment Superresponse

Munger opens his expanded 1995–2005 masterwork with the tendency he considers most powerful and most underrated, even by people who think they already understand it: reward and punishment superresponse. People respond to incentives far more strongly than almost anyone expects, and virtually nothing else predicts behavior as reliably.

Two business cases make the point. FedEx could not get its overnight hub to finish sorting packages on time — until management stopped paying night-shift workers by the hour and paid them by the shift, letting them go home when the work was done. The problem vanished immediately. At Xerox, founder Joe Wilson had to return from government service to figure out why an inferior older machine was outselling a better new one: salesmen earned higher commissions on the worse product.

The most dangerous consequence is incentive-caused bias. A decent person's cognition drifts, without conscious dishonesty, toward conclusions that serve his own interests — the surgeon who removes healthy gall bladders sincerely believing they are diseased, the consultant whose report always recommends more consulting. Munger's rules: fear professional advice when it is especially good for the adviser, and trust nothing but your own checked reasoning when the adviser's incentives conflict with yours. Ben Franklin's insight applies — if you would persuade, appeal to interest, not to reason.

Incentive design therefore deserves obsessive attention. Sound systems make cheating hard, as the cash register did more for commercial morality than any sermon. Cost-plus contracts, by contrast, practically invite abuse. Munger also endorses granny's rule as personal discipline: force yourself to do the unpleasant necessary task first, and only then the pleasant one — daily.

Punishment works too, though less well than reward. Prompt, clear consequences change behavior; Munger likes the old Roman arrangement in which a bridge's builder stood beneath the arch as its scaffolding was removed — accountability that concentrated engineering minds wonderfully.
The Emotional Distorters: Liking, Hating, and Envy

Three tendencies warp judgment through raw feeling. Liking/loving tendency makes people ignore the faults of, and comply with the wishes of, those they like — and favor people, products, and actions merely associated with the object of affection. This creates feedback loops: liking leads to favors, favors deepen liking. Advertisers and salespeople exploit it relentlessly, which is why attractive, likable people sell things.

Its mirror image, disliking/hating tendency, makes people ignore virtues of those they dislike, hate everything associated with the disliked person, and distort facts to justify the hatred. Munger notes that war-level hatred can make people believe almost anything about an enemy; the same machinery, at lower intensity, corrupts everyday disputes, litigation, and politics. The practical danger is that both tendencies operate below consciousness — you do not feel yourself weighing evidence unfairly.

Envy/jealousy tendency deserves special contempt in Munger's view. It drove sibling rivalry in the Old Testament, drives compensation wars in modern firms, and wrecks otherwise sensible institutions when someone learns what a peer earns. He endorses Buffett's observation that envy, not greed, drives the world — people can tolerate being poor far more easily than being poorer than a neighbor. Munger finds it remarkable that envy is nearly absent from psychology textbooks despite its enormous role in human misery, a gap he attributes to the taboo against naming it.

The antidotes are structural, not heroic. Design compensation systems anticipating envy rather than hoping it away. When judging anyone you strongly like or dislike, deliberately argue the other side. And treat envy as the one deadly sin that is no fun at all — pure cost, no pleasure — so the rational move is simply to refuse to play. (Chapter 3's Johnny Carson misery prescriptions make the same point by inversion.)
The Rush to Certainty: Doubt-Avoidance, Inconsistency-Avoidance, and Curiosity

Doubt-avoidance tendency is the brain's programmed hurry to eliminate uncertainty by reaching a decision — any decision. It evolved sensibly (a threatened animal cannot afford lengthy deliberation) and is triggered by the combination of puzzlement and stress, which is exactly why religions flourish and why courts force judges and juries into procedures that delay decision until evidence is heard. An unthreatened person can and should wait.

Far more consequential is inconsistency-avoidance tendency: the mind's extreme reluctance to change its habits, loyalties, identities, and prior conclusions. Bad habits persist because the chains are too light to be felt until too heavy to be broken; Munger's advice is to prevent them rather than plan to break them. The tendency also produces first-conclusion bias — Munger compares the mind to an egg that seals itself after one sperm enters: the first idea in blocks all later ones. Keynes saw the same thing in his famously educated colleagues: the difficulty was never accepting new ideas but escaping old ones.

Consistency pressure has a constructive exploit and a destructive one. Constructively, Franklin converted a hostile legislator into an ally by asking to borrow a rare book — people come to like those they have helped, reasoning backward from their own act. Destructively, hazing and painful initiation rites make new members value the group more, because admitting the suffering was pointless would be intolerable; cults weaponize the same machinery. Whatever you publicly commit to, you will defend — so be slow to pound in your own conclusions.

Against these stands curiosity tendency, one of the few native forces that counteracts the others. Curiosity gave Athens its science and gives modern individuals a way to keep learning past formal education, correcting bad conclusions the other tendencies would happily preserve; Munger treats lifelong curiosity as a load-bearing antidote, not a luxury.
Social Reflexes Manipulators Love: Fairness, Reciprocation, and Mere Association

Kantian fairness tendency is the human expectation of, and willingness to supply, fair conduct — behavior that would work if everyone followed it, echoing Kant's categorical imperative. Strangers alternate courteously in highway merges with no enforcer in sight, and people will accept small personal injustices to preserve a system that is fairer overall. The flip side: perceived unfairness triggers disproportionate hostility, so institutions that violate fairness norms breed lasting rage.

Reciprocation tendency — returning both favors and disfavors, often with interest — is among the most exploitable reflexes. Robert Cialdini's concession experiment is the canonical demonstration: when researchers asked students to chaperone juvenile delinquents on a zoo trip, one in six agreed. When they first made an outrageous request — two years of weekly counseling — and, after refusal, "retreated" to the zoo trip, compliance tripled to half. The requester's concession compelled a reciprocal concession. The same mechanism powers car salesmen's small courtesies, industrial buyers corrupted by vendors' gifts (which is why Sam Walton banned purchasing agents from accepting so much as a lunch), and negotiators everywhere. Reciprocation also has a noble side — it underwrites trade, marriage, and the spontaneous WWI trench truces — and guilt, in Munger's view, is what you feel when reciprocate-favor programming collides with reward superresponse.

Influence-from-mere-association tendency extends Pavlov beyond dogs: we judge things by what they merely accompany. Advertisers pair products with beautiful imagery; buyers infer quality from high price alone, so raising prices sometimes raises sales. Past success is a treacherous associator — a man who won his last few bets under certain conditions concludes the conditions caused the wins. The nastiest variant is Persian messenger syndrome: hating the bearer of bad news, as ancient kings killed messengers and as CBS under Bill Paley decayed once subordinates learned to bring only welcome tidings. The antidote is an explicit culture of "bad news first," and disciplined checking of whether an association is actually causal.
The Flattering Mirror: Denial and Excessive Self-Regard

Simple, pain-avoiding psychological denial is the distortion of reality until it becomes bearable. Munger's stark example is a family friend who insisted her athlete son, lost at sea, was alive — reality was intolerable, so the mind rejected it. Some denial in grief is forgivable; the unforgivable version appears in addiction, where alcoholics and addicts deny deterioration precisely when clear sight matters most. Munger's derived rule is absolute avoidance of any chemical with addictive potential, because denial will disable the alarm system exactly when it is needed.

Excessive self-regard tendency is broader and sneakier. People systematically overrate themselves: in Swedish surveys, 90% of drivers judged themselves above average. They overvalue their possessions — the endowment effect — and prize their own conclusions as possessions too, which supercharges first-conclusion bias. Lottery players value tickets bearing self-picked numbers far above random ones, though the odds are identical. Hiring committees overweight vivid face-to-face impressions against boring but predictive track records — Munger suggests trusting the record over the interview.

Self-regard also manufactures self-serving moral accounting. Munger calls it the Tolstoy effect, after Tolstoy's observation that the worst criminals do not see themselves as bad: they either deny the crime or excuse it as an understandable response to their circumstances. Institutions must not tolerate either move, because unfixed excuse-making metastasizes; the right regime is Navy-style strictness where the stakes are high — the captain whose ship goes aground is finished, fair or not, because the standard protects everyone.

The working antidote is forcing objectivity on yourself — treating your own opinions with the skepticism you would apply to a stranger's, and remembering that the person easiest for you to fool is you.
Wishful Thinking and the Sting of Loss: Overoptimism and Deprival Superreaction

Overoptimism tendency shows that pain-avoidance is not needed for distortion — people display excess optimism even when things are going well. Munger reaches back to Demosthenes: "What a man wishes, that also will he believe." Wishing corrupts probability estimates in business plans, market forecasts, and personal choices alike. The antidote is not a mood but a method: trained, habitual use of the elementary probability math of Fermat and Pascal (chapter 1's foundational model), forcing wishes to survive arithmetic.

Its dark twin is deprival-superreaction tendency: losses, and even threatened or narrowly missed gains, hurt far more than equivalent gains please. The Munger family dog was a perfectly agreeable animal with one exception — try to take food from its mouth and it would bite the hand that fed it. Humans are the same about property, status, territory, and opinions. A man who loses $10 he nearly possessed feels robbed of what was "his," which is why slot machines and video poker are engineered to shower players with near misses, each one triggering deprival superreaction and another coin.

The tendency explains large-scale business disasters. Labor unions destroyed employers rather than accept wage takebacks, because giving up what workers already had felt unbearable even when refusal meant losing everything. Coca-Cola's New Coke fiasco was a deprival lollapalooza: taking away the old formula provoked a national fury no taste test predicted. Deprival superreaction also feeds gambling escalation — doubling down to erase a loss — and makes people defend threatened ideologies with irrational ferocity. Combined with commitment pressure at open-outcry auctions, it produces ruinous overbidding (chapter 6 dissects that lollapalooza).

Antidotes: never let what you paid or what you almost had drive the next decision; evaluate positions as if starting fresh. And follow Munger's auction rule — the best defense against a stupid bidding war is not attending.
In practice
Before judging any advice, decision, or organizational failure this week, write down who gets paid for what — then ask whether the conclusion would survive a change in incentives.
Catch one first conclusion and deliberately argue the opposite case for ten minutes before letting yourself decide; treat your opinions as things to test, not possessions to defend.
Apply granny's rule daily: do the most unpleasant necessary task first, and schedule the enjoyable work as the reward.
When someone concedes or does you a small favor in a negotiation, pause and check whether your urge to reciprocate is about to cost more than the favor was worth.
Tell your team explicitly that bad news must arrive first and fast, and visibly reward the next person who brings it.
Price one decision as if you owned nothing: ask what you would pay or do starting from scratch, ignoring sunk costs, near misses, and what you "almost had."
